turns.label: Turn 1
turns.briefing: Complete Turn 1. Use the reference items on the desk.

questions.prompt: You have a table customers(customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, signup_date). A colleague asks for every column for all customers who signed up on or after 2023-01-01. Which SQL statement returns the correct rows? Use the table.
options: SELECT ALL FROM customers WHERE signup_date = '2023-01-01';
SELECT customer_id, first_name FROM customers WHERE signup_date > '2023-01-01';
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE signup_date BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND NOW();

questions.prompt: Using the same customers table, write a short SQL query to return customer_id and email for customers whose email ends with '@example.com'. Use the table.
questions.expected: SELECT customer_id, email FROM customers WHERE email LIKE '%@example.com';
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT customer_id, email FROM customers WHERE email LIKE '%@example.com';

questions.prompt: Now you add orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount). You need a list of customers and their total order amount across all orders. Which JOIN and aggregation approach is best? Use the customers and orders tables.
options: SELECT c.customer_id, SUM(o.total_amount) FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id;
SELECT c.customer_id, COALESCE(SUM(o.total_amount),0) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id;
SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(o.total_amount) FROM orders o GROUP BY o.customer_id;
SELECT DISTINCT c.customer_id, o.total_amount FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id=o.customer_id;
questions.expected: SELECT c.customer_id, COALESCE(SUM(o.total_amount),0) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id;

questions.prompt: You must report how many orders each month had total_amount > 100. Given orders(order_date, total_amount), which query correctly groups by month and counts qualifying orders? Use the orders table.
options: SELECT MONTH(order_date) AS month, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE total_amount > 100 GROUP BY MONTH(order_date);
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE total_amount > 100 GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date);
SELECT TO_CHAR(order_date,'MM') AS month, SUM(total_amount>100) FROM orders GROUP BY TO_CHAR(order_date,'MM');
SELECT order_date, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE total_amount > 100 GROUP BY order_date;
questions.expected: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE total_amount > 100 GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date);

questions.prompt: A product_views table(view_id, product_id, user_id, viewed_at) exists. Write a query that returns product_id and the number of unique users who viewed it in the last 7 days, but only for products where that count exceeds the average unique viewers across all products in the same 7-day window. Use product_views and consider current_date as reference.
questions.expected: WITH recent AS ( SELECT product_id, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS viewers FROM product_views WHERE viewed_at >= current_date - INTERVAL '7 days' GROUP BY product_id ) SELECT r.product_id, r.viewers FROM recent r WHERE r.viewers > (SELECT AVG(viewers) FROM recent);
questions.idealAnswer: WITH recent AS ( SELECT product_id, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS viewers FROM product_views WHERE viewed_at >= current_date - INTERVAL '7 days' GROUP BY product_id ) SELECT r.product_id, r.viewers FROM recent r WHERE r.viewers > (SELECT AVG(viewers) FROM recent);

turns.label: Turn 3
turns.briefing: Complete Turn 3. Use the reference items on the desk.

questions.prompt: Sales team asks for a report: list customers whose monthly spending is above the average monthly spending for that customer across all months. The schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount). Explain and write a correlated subquery that returns customer_id, month, month_total where month_total is that month's total and is greater than that customer's average monthly total. Evidence required: use the orders table.
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT o.customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, SUM(o.total_amount) AS month_total FROM orders o GROUP BY o.customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) HAVING SUM(o.total_amount) > ( SELECT AVG(month_sum) FROM ( SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o2.order_date) AS m2, SUM(o2.total_amount) AS month_sum FROM orders o2 WHERE o2.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', o2.order_date) ) t );

questions.prompt: You need to pick the top 3 orders by total_amount for each customer using orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount). Describe how to use a window function to produce order_rank and return only ranks 1-3. Provide the SQL and brief justification. Evidence required: use the orders table.
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount FROM ( SELECT o.*, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY total_amount DESC) AS rn FROM orders o ) t WHERE rn <= 3;

questions.prompt: Database performance issue: table orders has 50M rows; queries filtering by order_date and customer_id are slow. Which indexing strategy gives the best general improvement for those filters and why? Use the orders table.
options: Create a composite B-tree index on (customer_id, order_date) because it supports queries filtering by customer first then date ranges efficiently.
Create separate single-column indexes on customer_id and order_date; the optimizer will always combine them for best performance.
Create a full-text index on total_amount and order_date to speed up numeric range queries.
No index; rely on sequential scan because indexes slow down inserts and won't help read performance.
questions.expected: Create a composite B-tree index on (customer_id, order_date) because it supports queries filtering by customer first then date ranges efficiently.

questions.prompt: You're preparing a deployment plan to optimize a slow aggregation query that groups orders by product_id and computes SUM(total_amount). Put these steps in the recommended order. Evidence required: use the orders table and explainors.
sequencePool: Add or adjust appropriate indexes (e.g., on product_id).
Rewrite the query to reduce data scanned (use WHERE, pre-aggregated tables, or partitioning).
Run the query again and compare metrics.
Consider materialized view or summary table if still slow.
Capture the slow query plan and execution metrics.
expected: Capture the slow query plan and execution metrics.
Add or adjust appropriate indexes (e.g., on product_id).
Rewrite the query to reduce data scanned (use WHERE, pre-aggregated tables, or partitioning).
Run the query again and compare metrics.
Consider materialized view or summary table if still slow.

Using the same customers table, write a short SQL query to return customer_id and email for customers whose email ends with '@example.com'. Use the table.
